text.fullText: From: bethtamminen@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/10/2026 12:06:54 PM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from bethtamminen@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I am taking the opportunity to communicate my strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water. In Minnesota we have spent decades cleaning up waters so polluted by earlier industry that we could not eat the fish or even swim in the rivers and lakes so affected. The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. The EPA’s proposed certification rules would decimate our ability to continue our work of cleaning waters for our state and region. Instead, it serves the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and harmful due to these reasons: 1. ) the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Tribes in Minnesota have been at the forefront of cleaning up the waters in our state. 2. ) the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. 3. ) the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification,making it impossible for setting conditions or denying or Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. 4. ) the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year - even when more time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes. This is a consolidation of federal power. It would deprive more local, informed groups of the authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. We have existing rules under the Clean Water Act that empower the communities affected by the quality of water to do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water. We need clean water to drink, swim in, and fish. Thank you. Sincerely, Ms. Beth Tamminen 502 Madison Ave Duluth, MN 55811-5928 bethtamminen@yahoo.com
